commentBody: A can-do Wednesday
We have been very nicely treated to some lovely recycling!
This was truly an outstanding puzzle
I hope everyone CAN CAN CAN CAN finish the puzzle today, since it wasn't too difficult.

Back in 1963, I took a tour of a factory in Copenhagen where they put up those little hams in heart-shaped cans. The guide, a pretty young woman in her early twenties, gave her remarks in English, French, German, and Italian. About halfway through the tour, she said, "Here in Denmark, we have a saying: We eat what we can, and what we can't, we can, and what we can't can, we export." She then went to say, in French, German, and Italian that she couldn't repeat what she had just said in English, but I think they all got it anyway because they laughed when she said it.

Got the puzzle done in 5:27.

DIdn't understand the circled letters or the shaded ones. Fortunately, it wasn't necessary.
Very nicely done
Clever! Took a minute to understand the shades clues, but glad I worked it out! Nicely done.
Very enjoyable puzzle with a clever theme!
Don't forget REFUSE!
A perfect Earth Day puzzle.

I CAN safely say that this puzzle was anagrammatically correct.  Having GAELIC and CELTIC identically clued was cute too.  There were so many gimmicks CRAMmed into this one I couldn’t keep track of them all.  Tons of fun.

I really enjoyed the theme.  Great aha moment with the "REDUCE, REUSE, RECYCLE".

A couple of the filler clues I thought had problems.  Like YEOW is a weird spelling for a cry of pain and had some tricky crossings.   I dismissed some correct answers to the crossings because I didn't think YEOW was a word.  Then LOUCHE crossed with HOPI, two very obscure references.

The theme clues with the anagrams and the shorter and longer versions of the answers were awesome.  Just so much unguessable niche trivia in the filler.
First time I'm moved enough to comment how amazing this puzzle is!  Great job!
Genius-level puzzle.  Well done.

Kangaroo words *and* two-anagrams-in-one (*) words in one puzzle!  Wow! Fun!
 
*there should a name for these. Should we just call them “teammate words”?
Clever clever clever. Easily my favourite Wednesday of the year.
Solved without seeing a theme. Now I’ll go look for it!

Fine puzzle, with all kinds of fun stuff going on.  But boy did I feel mentally sluggish.  I really thought I was going to have to cry uncle, but somehow I got through it.

My excuse is that I was flummoxed because I had a small leak in a basement pipe.  In trying to tighten the fittings, I made it into a large leak.  This is the extent of my plumbing skills--to turn a small leak into a large leak.

Grand Marais is a gorgeous little town, but it can be tough to find a plumber.  I was lucky enough to get one who'd retired.  He managed to get it stopped.

I told him I was hopeless at home repair.  He said, "But I bet you're good at numbers."  That is true.  However, being able to prove that the square root of 2 is an irrational number doesn't really impress a leaking pipe all that much.

Regarding the dust up about the profane word in the puzzle yesterday, the one which was eventually removed.  I think I may have gone off half-cocked, as I am wont to do.  I hope I didn't make any new enemies, or reinforce old ones.
This puzzle has a lot going on.

Excellent, all praise, to come up with 2 forms of reuse & recycling (& reducing, since the embedded word is shorter), WOW!

Uncanny to see Odin so soon, as well as Helen and the Trojans (and Trojans in their prophylactic sense are among that class of products where it is best NOT to reuse, reduce, or perhaps even recycle)... and thank heavens we had en banc recently enough it was still in my head! But in the economy of the Times puzzles this makes sense: they are all recycled words or phrases. In fact, all the fill in this puzzle has been used previously with the exception of VMAS, a debut (xwordinfo); conservationism is alive and well at the NYT.

Some cogent Agee quotes from LUNPFM, his journey through 1930s poverty America, accompanied by Walker Evans.

“And a human being whose life is nurtured in an advantage which has accrued from the disadvantage of other human beings, and who prefers that this should remain as it is, is a human being by definition only, having much more in common with the bedbug, the tapeworm, the cancer, and the scavengers of the deep sea.”

“This is why the camera seems to me, next to unassisted and weaponless consciousness, the central instrument of our time; and is why in turn I feel such rage at its misuse: which has spread so nearly universal a corruption of sight that I know of less than a dozen alive whose eyes I can trust even so much as my own.”

“The world is full of people who have never, since childhood, met an open doorway.”
Did I miss it or did you neglect to mention that 21 and 28 across are palindromes? I thought that's why the last three letters were shaded in those two answers.

Wow, the constructor in me is just stanning. I can’t remember when I’ve seen this dense of theme material. I loved your sweet constructor’s note, Hoang, but what I really want is a geek out about how you came up with this idea, how hard it was to implement, and what was going through your brain while you were creating it. I feel like I’m looking at a Dali or Bosch painting that you can look at for hours and still marvel at the details. 3 reveals. The appropriate use of CAN as the reused word. The  smoothness of the reduced words that so precisely mirror their longer version. The elegance of the recycling words that use the same letters again exactly and no more. 
Maybe this is more of a constructor’s puzzle but I hope the allure to the solver is the slow burn of gradually revealing the themes and wondering how they will all fit together. 
Two more unrelated notes: I guess they wanted a head start on REUSE when they reused FLY yesterday? And second I hadn’t heard of LOUCHE and I had everything but the L at first—I guarantee you it wasn’t the first letter that came to mind.

Fabulous puzzle in celebration of Earth Day. 

I love the multi-layer theme and the bonus inclusion of environmentalist Jane GOODALL.

Well done, Hoang-Kim. I hope everyone is encouraged to follow the motto and do their part to save our planet.
Nice recycling for Earth Day!
very impressive
Very cool puzzle—such neat twists on the theme. Happy Earth Day!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
A really lush and diverting trek of standard Wednesday difficulty.

Very novel grid.

Everything about this puzzle was top class. 
The earth day appropriate theme. 
The Easter eggs for each part of reuse, reduce, recycle. 

The constructor notes. 
Everything that I expect and treasure about a NYT xword. 

Thank you
Hoang-Kim vu
Some of you guys are very easily impressed.

Wait, you're not allowed to reus..... OHHHHH.

Bravo.
I'm sure there was something very clever going on with the theme here, but it was faster and easier to just solve it as a themeless.
Happy Earth Day! Loved the puzzle and the theme.

This puzzle was awe-inspiring, much like our planet.

Thank you for the multi-layered work of art, Hoang-Kim.

Happy Earth Day to all!
—Let's TEND to it with TENDerness.
One of the most thoughtful, well-designed puzzles I have ever seen. Kudos on a remarkable creation @ Hoang-Kim Vu and Happy Earth Day everyone!!
Very clever and enjoyable!
Lil rippa of a puzz! Thx. BEDSLATS - Brill!

Pretty clever after the fact!
I think I would have solved 15% faster if I could have removed the circles and shading. Those are just distractions in this puzzle.
Enjoyable though. Appropriate difficulty.
Have Wednesday’s Wordle and Connections dropped yet? I’m out of country, two hours ahead. They drop at midnight right?

"This is without a doubt one of the most complex themes I’ve ever seen in a puzzle without a rebus."

Complex? I'd say lousy, but to each their own. And DAUBS? Be so for real

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
This puzzle raises the age-old esthetic tussle (get piker moi who's been at the Times puzzle but a year and change talking about age-old tussles):  If the theme and all its gimmicks add little to streamlining the time and effort of the solve, why bother with the theme at all?

And, as ever, I continue to come down on the side of pro-theme/pro-gimmick for the sake of the setter's personal delight and verbal flexing at the very least.

Like Nietzsche said:  "...for it is only as an aesthetic phenomenon that existence and the world are eternally justified".
Not a good effort on my part.  Got stuck at the end and took *forever* to find my mistake.  A minute slower than my Wednesday average, and my average is already bad.  :-(
Don’t miss Mr Vu’s constructor notes today. This was a pretty special package: amazing puzzle, Isaac Aronow column, great notes. Even zee orchestra is beautiful!

A masterpiece! Congratulations on a fantastic theme idea that was brilliantly executed. The GRIN spread across my face as I uncovered more and more layers. This is my favorite puzzle of the year thus far.
I get the puzzle, but don't get the the photo of the snail. How is it connected to the puzzle?
Oh, those repeats! I was second guessing myself all the way through. Nice one!

Very clever theme, although I didn't actually use it during the solve (except to immediately enter the 3 R's at the beginning). Nice eclectic way of theming each different R-word, great idea!
I finished under my Wednesday average, unlike most commenters here today, only a few actors/sports/US college clues tripped me up.
Just noticed this got me a 101-day streak too - let me just say a big thanks to all the constructors and editors and everyone else involved in providing me with such an enjoyable daily hobby!
Amazing theme. Only could appreciate it after it was over, so it made the solve harder with all them CANs. Extremely complex, but satisfying in the end. Kudos!
This one is a gem.
Amusing theme. Pretty tired of "slats" though.
Theme was irrelevant after reduce reuse recycle, and there was a lot of obscure words and proper nouns. Wasn't really a fan

What a delight for Earth Day! A tightly constructed, intricate set of interlocking theme clues where nothing felt forced, no bizarre usages - well done, Mr. Vu. I was pleased to finish just under my average.

Happy Earth Day. Talk about Reduce, Reuse, Recycle! Read "Let Us Now Praise Famous Men" to learn about the era of the Dust Bowl of the 1930s. Poor agricultural practices contributed to the disaster. You must see the two-part documentary "The Dust Bowl" by Ken Burns on PBS. It's amazing. https://www.thirteen.org/programs/the-dust-bowl/ Here's another documentary about the Dust Bowl. https://www.pbs.org/video/is-the-dust-bowl-happening-again-o3tem4/ Plant some flowers today!

Did I need the theme to solve the puzzle?

No, but I don't need the items that decorate my home either.

Both just make life nicer.

This was such a densely packed theme, but the fill didn't suffer for it.

Using CAN as the recycled word felt like a bonus in an already remarkable puzzle.
I can dig that the CAN placements mirrored the legs in the can-can dance

Masterpiece. A perfect puzzle.

In one Wednesday grid, we get a 3-part quote. Cool.

Each word in the quote functions as an action and a theme. Related to the original quote. Whoa.

Each of the 3 themes then performs its own specific action, 'creating' words found on the grid. No. Way.

Really enjoyed this. Massive respect for the idea and the landing. This is one I'll clear from time to time in the archive and do it again.
I'm left slack-jAWED by this inGENIoUS puZZle!

Very clever.  In fact, clever enough for a Thursday I'd say.  I should have gone into this one with a CAN do attitude which may have helped me see the final trick, REUSE, but alas I had to check puzzle.
Loved it! Very satisfying solve :-)
This was a great puzzle! I was able to solve it without focusing on the theme answers but when it was completed (below my average time), I really appreciated how elegantly it was designed.
What a blast!
I loved this puzzle! And the constructor’s note made me all the more enamored of it. A great mix of challenge and entertainment. Well done!

Got a wee bit dizzy
trying to keep up with
the triple theme, 

but it was so worth it.

What a feat of construction!

A perfect Wednesday, for me.

Happy Earth Day!

Well, for a word-quirk loving guy, I was in heaven over this one with its: 
•	Words that contain their synonyms.
•	Words whose second halves use the same letters as their first halves.
•	Rare-in-crosswords five-letter palindrome (SHAHS), and rarer-in crosswords six-letter palindrome (PULLUP).

I was agog at how much theme Hoang-Kim fit into the box, and how he was able to get the words with circles – which I’m thinking are hard to come by, to begin with – symmetrical.  

Plus, this was a very sweet solve.  Realizing what the words with circles and words with gray did brought happy pings.  Beauty, with SLIVER, ENAMOR, and LOUCHE, warmed my heart.  I also liked seeing the rhyming HOPI and ROPY.

Just a playground of a solve, Hoang-Kim, and thank you.  Congratulations, too, on hitting the cycle (a puzzle for each day of the week)!

Both elegant and chewy, I really enjoyed this. The theme appeared quickly, but there were enough sticky corners to slow me down a tad.  The last letter to each of 28, 29 and 30D just wouldn’t come as I didn’t know any of the answers. It was a face palm moment when I finally got CAN after Isaac pointed out the multiple use of the word. 
Another sticking point for me was BETSY. Not a common derivative here, so I had the more familiar Betty. Didn’t notice that ENOt wasn’t right. Duh. 
A lovely construction overall.

Laid up today with a crook back. I was mid Alpaca poo pick when it went so had to hobble the 3 fields back before I could collapse on the sofa.  At least I can sit with the Games without feeling guilty.
What a delight! I felt a smile spread across my face as the theme emerged with a wait! What day is it? Loved it!
This was one of my favorites so far! (And on my Earth Day birthday, no less!) It felt like it was 3D after the denouement! ❤️

lol!! BED SLATS and LUGE are harder than ENOS? I have a copy of War & Peace sitting on my shelf of bucket list fiction reading. The bible not so much. My Achilles heel in crossword puzzles. C'est la vie. I chose BETTY instead of BETSY and then I was stuck trying to figure out why I didn't hear the jaunty solved music. Enjoyed anyway after figuring out the theme on my own. Ciao!
Yes we CAN, CAN, CAN, CAN. CAN we? The Pointer Sisters say we CAN. https://youtu.be/IzZkjjENj_w?si=tjkxBVPihW9e-_Zj

Not exactly enamorY; kind of ROPY, but more CANNY and at times, GRINNY.  I felt like I was at the cabaret, or somewhere. I enjoyed it. The easy ones were very easy, and the not-so-easy ones... well... not so easy.

The crosses of 13D and 26A slowed me down immensely. Further, 12D not being REFUSE, was a great help. I had no idea what a lousse or a lousce was, and will probably never add LOUCHE in my vocabulary. 

I surely thought Elissa over ELISHA as a woman's name. I'm guessing it's *not* pronounced like the OT prophet who succeeded Elijah? Rhymes with Tricia? But, after looking up the referenced movie, (sheesh, what a story line), I'll have to pass on that one, sorry. Elisha looks to have been in more than one edgy role, I see.
Wow. This may be the cleverest puzzle I've ever done. Not too hard, but so ingenious & satisfying!
Good one! The theme was interesting and actually helped me figure out a few of the answers. More like this, please!
Hardly got any of the pop culture references. Am I just too young?
Whoops, thought 1A was from Rush. Too much going on in this puzzle. Colors, circles, shades, embedded words, etc. Easy, but I lost interest.
Like a Sunday on a Wednesday! :-)
I finished this on the CAN in well under my average time.  Didn’t work out the shades/circles whatever.

That was incredible!

And I can't help but note the synchronicity as I was just noting how beautiful Earth is as I looked down on it from my private jet!

Brilliant! Simply brilliant. So much going on that fits a tight theme. It astounds me how creative these crossword constructors can be to fit so much wordplay into such a tight puzzle. Thanks for giving me a smile today!

This puzzle took a little persistence, but I completed it without lookups and in a decent amount of time.

As I did the puzzle, I said to myself: "So what, exactly is the distinction between GAELIC and CELTIC"?  So I looked it up. It was a little involved for someone only halfway through their morning coffee, but I managed it.

Then my youngest son walked into the room and started talking about using quantum mechanics to search for an individual's heartbeat via the faint electrical signals it gives off. I looked that up too (WAY out of my depth), and we talked about it a while.

So I have this little headache now, and I'm grinding my teeth a bit.
The fun in this puzzle even includes tapping the three key answers (in the app) just to see the related ones light up in sets.
Wonderful puzzle today with great vocabulary…thank you …more please just like this
It’s April and we already have a contender for “2026 Best.”

Amazing! Such a complex theme, executed in a way that's clear and coherent. I liked the bonus REUSE of the clue "Like some Scots."

There was plenty of fun fill as well, from ENAMOR to LOUCHE to HEROD. This puzzle put a GRIN on my face!

Excuse me while I resuse some superlatives:

Very very very cool puzzle!

Whew. And Wow! Tough one early on for me, but finally tumbling to the trick was the huge turning point and this was one amazing piece of construction. Thumbs up. And... appropriate puzzle find today. A Sunday from June 21, 2015 by Timothy Polin with the title: "Climbing the corporate ladder." Hard to describe this one, but I'll take a shot at it. A couple of examples: 5A. ______TESTS ______E ______S ______L 34A.TRUEFA No clue for 5a. 5d was straightforwardly clued as the car and... 34a was straightforwardly clued with no hint as to it climbing up and across. A couple of other examples: ____STAMP ____E ____G POSTA ____SPECT ____E ____R ____T INTHA ____ALLACY ____F ____L ____A LOGIC And the down crossing parts were also clued separately, eg...SEGA, SERTA, AFLAC And there were more. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=6/21/2015&g=34&d=A ....
CAN do!
Wonderfully fun puzzle, with a perfect theme for Earth Day! Kudos, Hoang-Kim Vu!

How great to come here and see that I am just adding my kudos to a large pile. 
Learned LOUCHE - the day is not wasted.
Loved the clue for BEDSLATS.  I have one bed with them (a twin), but it's old. I thought platforms were more the thing these days, even supplanting box springs. 
I've been recycling since the '70s - even tearing the glassine out of envelopes, and the cellophane out of pasta boxes. Soooo depressing that they haven't found a way make it really work even 50 years on. (Oh my...50 years? Now I really want to crawl back in my hole!)
An interesting theme, well executed but YEOW did that last little SW corner get me….

What a fun puzzle! I’m in awe at the nested themes here and I LOVE IT. I’ve only just started constructing and this is one of those that just made me sit back and say “wow I hope one day I can make something as elegant as this”

ALSO the inclusion of LOUCHE for 26A was very enjoyable! Reminded me of my host mom in France. I went out on a date with a French man while studying abroad and she warned me to be careful of “Les louches” which is slang (as I understand it) for playboys or shady figures in France. Combine that with the clue of “Rakish” and I was kicking my feet and giggling. 

Thank you!!
I love, love, loved this puzzle - so clever and multi-layered.  And it is so nice to see a comments column where ALL the comments (well, with maybe a rare exception) were positive!!

Wow! 🤩 This is definitely in my POY folder. I had the grid completed, but no gold star. I finally realized that I had Cut instead of CAN for [Ax]. I had a boss named BETSY, so that was my first guess for 49D. I’m so impressed by the construction of this puzzle. Thank you, Hoang-Kim Vu!! Happy Earth Day, Crosslandia.
Okay now, this is just too clever for words! The squares were easy to fill in but I didn't grasp the theme and subthemes until afterward. Wow. Hoang-Kim Vu, you are brainy!
FABULOUS puzzle!
For all Huang-Kim pulled off in his sterling execution of this multi-layered theme, I loved that the overriding takeaway of his notes was humility, wonder, and gratitude.

LOVED IT! I got all the clues except for the anagrams. After reading explanation I went back and stared at the puzzle for a long minute. The loud sound you heard was a palm to the forehead smack when I finally got it. Brilliant.
Such a delight!  Well done
Been doing crosswords a long time and have to say this is one of the most impressive constructions I’ve seen.  Well done!

Jeopardy Jamie watch: 
Jamie usually goes into Final Jeopardy with an enormous lead. It doesn't matter if he gets the answer right or wrong. But last night was a different story. Another contestant could have replaced him as champion--if Jamie had gotten the answer wrong. The question was about a person who requested a song to be composed even though it wouldn't apply to him as he was facing the guillotine. The answer: "The Marseillaise" and all three contestants got the answer right. Jamie bet just enough to remain champion (by $1.00.).

There were two answers I knew last night that none of the players knew!! One was about a fluid sometimes used with coolant in cars: antifreeze. The other was about the renowned British nun who was also an art critic: Sister Wendy!

By the way, Jamie was resplendent last night in a sport jacket, shirt and tie with an orange pocket square. 

Jamie is among the top few players on Jeopardy now. The other day he said he likes to do Cryptic crosswords.

I am always flabbergasted at his wide range of knowledge. How can anyone know all that???
Can anyone tell me the meaning of “esh”  and “lup”? I enjoyed the puzzle and finished it 9 minutes faster than my average, but I am stumped by the meaning of these shaded letters on21 and 28 across.
Awesome.

i am EN(AMORe)d with this puzzle. It's GOODALL over.

Seriously, enjoyed it, the slow unfolding of the theme, and the musing to see how the circles and shades worked the theme.
Thanks, Hoang-Kim Vu.

Biggest concern locally is the fire in Brantley County (south Georgia). We've had thick acrid smoke here on the coast for several days, and no rain in the forecast. The fire jumped across highway 82 yesterday and now is up to 5000 acres. Homes burned, people evacuating. Extra concern for a horse'n'dog rescue with 200 animals to relocate.

Incredibly clever, tri-partite theme with a triple revealer makes for an excellent Wednesday puzzle!

The only part of the theme that I noticed when I was solving was the repeated CANs, and I worried that that wasn't a very interesting theme.

But after completion, taking the time to savor REDUCE, REUSE, RECYCLE was a delight.
This is one of those times that I was glad there were circles and shading.  Would I have got the theme otherwise?  I doubt it.
And it was also nice that the relevant themers "lit up" when you clicked on each of the three parts of the revealers.

I wonder in passing whether those who solve on paper (or on stone tablets) enjoyed the theme?

Thanks, Hoang-Kim Vu for a delightful Wednesday!

Earth Day is also my mother’s birthday. First held in 1970, when my mom was 60. She once said she was officially older than dirt. 

I really liked the puzzle. I’m notoriously slow to catch on to themes but today was different. REDUCE had fallen early and I couldn’t stop grinning as I plugged along. 

Happy birthday! Mom!,

20 Across - a condition that may include stimming should be autism not ADHD. Although having both conditions is common, they are not the same. Stimming is generally used by autistic people. 
Happy Autism Awareness month. :)
This one was an absolute work of art.
gotta be a POTY candidate right? so dense and so well done!
Great puzzle! I'd like to repeat my request to enable a feature to search the archive by constructor.

A happy Lenin's Birthday to all who celebrate. And now I have that Schoolhouse Rock song stuck in my head.

Three...is a magic number.
The theme helped me out with REUSE, but the meaning of REDUCE and RECYCLE only became apparent after I finished. Still a fun puzzle though. Nice Wednesday. The streak lives on!
Okay, I had a weird moment with 9-Down.   With a partially filled grid I actually considered REDSTATE for the "Supporters of a king or queen".  Not happy that my brain went there.
Brilliant construction! Rather than relying on the tried & true (snooze) Earth Day theme, it was weaved into such clever wordplay. Loved it! Now I’m off to hug a 🌳 b/c Earth Day is everyday!

Of course I wanted WORKER BEES for 9D (my Grampa is still tending his hives in heaven, I'm pretty sure.)

As for the theme, which is great:  DHubby and I are now living in the least-RECYCLing-conscious state in the Union (although our city/county has a minimal program,) and we are visiting a city/state which is possibly THE most environmentally-conscious in the nation.  

There is an actress named ELISHA?  That was hard to do, but at last I was forced to enter the H.  That gave me LOUCHE, which is a word I do know (as is "Rakish,") and I object!  LOUCHE has connotations that do not apply to RAKISH, which can just mean "fetching" or "slightly daring."  
"He wore his hat at a rakish angle and sported a cane."
"Though handsome, he was the worse for wear, and one had the impression that his was a LOUCHE life-style."
I blame the clue editors.

The multiple levels of theme and wordplay are just wonderful.  Puzzle of the Year?  Definitely in the running, IMHO.

One of my favourite puzzles to date!! I loved how the themes were revealed - I got Reduce first but was really stumped by Recycle. Reuse was too cheeky. I loved it. Excellent Earth Day themed crossword!

Really loved this one, especially the cluing around the theme & sub-theme entries. The center section was pretty tough until I got the recycled clues about Scots, but the rest flowed on from there. Final solve time was a tad slower than usual but this puzzle was so satisfying to solve that I don't mind.

Wow. That took me 35 minutes, and for a bit I wasn't sure I would finish. Understood all the many thematic entries, but forever to finally get UPC, which I needed for confirmation since I didn't know LOUCHE or ELISHA.

Too shaken to comment further now. Happy Earth Day to those who celebrate.

Excellent puzzle, so very appropriate for Earth Day. FYI here, in Emmet County, MI. , we are blessed with one of the best recycling programs anywhere. A bustling Transfer Station, that repurposes many products, and a curbside pickup service, that is very user friendly.
Brilliant and efficient theme construction with lively fill. Fun and top-notch!
This was so clever! Thanks for the fun solve - happy Earth Day!
Well done! and fun!  Loved the two scots, louche, two cans!  and the Earth Day theme.
How? Just…how? Sooooo impressive! Absolutely loved it. What a work of art. And I learned a new word today too.
Wow.  Super fun solve, and the multi-layered theme is brilliant -- the mind boggles to think that anyone has come up with such an idea, let alone executed it so deftly.  Hats off!

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

the night of a thousand ravens

a/ in pain in fibrous grief
you emerge
s l o w l y
d/ to see the river of dreaming
to seep in
d r a i n i n g
d r a i n i n g
a/ a sliver
For 64A, I had GLAss instead of GLAZE and it totally kiln-ed my time! 🤭🤭🤭
This was so fun! I loved the theme!

I am kind of shocked at how well liked this puzzle seems to be.  I just don't appreciate all the clever frou-frou, and the light show, as much as most seem to do.  Go figure!  I am a big fan of Earth Day, though.  
Go Planet!
Happy Earth Day! Fun puzzle, appropriately themed.

It took me a minute to get all the elements of the theme, but once I did I really appreciated it.

Happy Earth Day! 🌎

And my usual late puzzle find. A Thursday from December 7, 1995 by Michael S. Maurer. The 15 letter answers across the middle: "Theme of this puzzle" FAMOUSLASTWORDS And other theme answers: COMEBACKSHANE THATSALLFOLKS NEVERMORE IMMELTING Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=12/7/1995&g=37&d=A ....

Great puzzle!

Side note, and I'm probably not the first to mention this, but in the web version the columns that are supposed to be highlighted with the revealer are not, and instead the wrong across rows are. I've noticed this many, many times over the last few weeks, in all three daily puzzles. Someone really ought to fix that.

I had a little trouble in the lower right corner that I had to look up the answer.  The answer for cry of pain could be a bit ambiguous, as I had “yelp.”  Then I had no idea what a ceramicist works with, so I couldn’t make any sense of the last two down answers in that corner.
I know I’m not the smartest or cleverest person here by a long shot, but the “Tricky Clues” often seems to answer ones I get easily and rarely the ones that stump me.

Wow!! What a terrific puzzle, more challenging than Wednesdays tend to be but it was such a joy to finally figure it all out. If I can find the time I might try some of his archived puzzles. But time is pretty short these days.
Please stay well and safe, everyone.
I think it's possible for a puzzle to be trying to do too much. This is the case here.
So cool! That was really fun, loved the multilayered theme and the earth day relevance.
Darn, I got hung up on Louche.  No gold star.  Good puzzle though, thanks.
Wow! What an amazing theme for a Wednesday. This was a very satisfying puzzle to solve. Thanks for the fun!
I am always, always, excited when I see Hoang-Kim Vu as a puzzle's constructor; once again, I have been awed. This was truly an excellent puzzle in every respect.

I wish I wasn't feeling as bad as I am, and as grumpy and fuzzy headed about it as I am, because I recognize this is a very cool and special puzzle all around, but I'm not feeling it. Oh well, sometimes we fake it till we make it. I'll see if I CAN! 

My fuzzy headedness is to a wild degree. Even with the GOOD in for Jane, and the fact that I absolutely know her and even got to hear her speak in person once, I could not pull the ALL out of my addled brain for ages. Same with HEROD. What!? For Pete sake, I went to a Christian college and Sunday school, etc. all my life! I always know my Bible answers. UGH!!

In the end, I had an error and I didn't have the spirit to look for it, so I did a check puzzle and found I'd put NaNA instead of NONA in the lower left. I'd have likely found it if I tried, especially since I do know how to spell ENAMOR, but it's freeing to not care about streaks. 

Anyhow, I want to be clear that this was a very, very cool puzzle and my less enthusiastic comment about it is all on me, not it. (It's me, no you!)

thanks for making a puzzle about the most important holiday of the year EARTH DAY  @glassjonespiano bandcamp and & YouTube Hunter college grad summa 2006  glassjonesphotos.com 
beautiful puzzle Hoang-Kim you're the only puzzle out there today that honors our planet and Jane Goodall brilliant puzzle in every way thanks!
Very gimmicky. Made me think it was Thursday for a moment.

Too late in the day, and too tired from work, to fully appreciate this jewel of a puzzle! I literally fell asleep while solving it ("What do you mean, the answer to 43D is 0000000?")

Picture perfect weather here, so off to celebrate the Day with a quick bike-ride:-)

I liked this one. It was a bit complicated if you think too hard about all the different tricks and I went back at the end to see how it all fit together. Close to my record in solving it though, so I guess the clues themselves were ones I knew pretty easily.
A very well crafted puzzle. 3 great mini themes. Definitely a marvel of construction. This should be in POY running

A very enjoyable and *packed* puzzle. In 100 years, I would never have understood what the mini-themes were, but I was delighted to read about them in Gameplay. I'm a better solver because of this puzzle.

Wow. 

I wish I don’t have to use more words. Sometimes less is more. 

Just wow.
I solved it with no lookups, but what anagram is LUP?

Puzzle was appropriately challenging for a Wednesday.  The theme easy, at least as far REDUCE, REUSE, RECYCLE are concerned.

The rest of the theme felt like a kitchen sink approach with a lot of everything thrown in. It feels like overkill.

I enjoyed the puzzle, but gave up on trying to make sense of all of the highlighted and circled squares.  Even the explanation in the Wordplay blog felt overly complicated.

Maybe this is all like missing the humor of an inside joke.
I got this puzzle, but I didn't enjoy it as much as so many here did, I'm jealous! I use "louche" often because it describes so many people I know . I'd never define it as "rakish", though.

While the construction of this puzzle seems impressive, I did not enjoy it at all. To me, this felt more like a Saturday. Broke my streak of 18 Wednesdays in a row (the first meaningful streak of Wednesdays I’ve had). Couldn’t figure out any of the west middle section. Had no idea what 28D, 29D, or 30D were. It’s just trivia you either know or don’t. So many Naticks! 12D? 62A? I get the shaded squares in 31A and 41A, but the other two don't make sense to me. LUP and ESH are words? Like @HeathieJ, I’m sick and grumpy and fuzzy-headed, but even if I weren’t, I can’t imagine liking this puzzle. 🤧😞
This puzzle mostly made me very happy, but YEOW made me sad.

One more find- a quite clever little puzzle. A Wednesday from September 16, 2009 by Maura B. Jacobson. Three 15 letter theme answers. Here they are with their clues: 17a - "Playground situation #1." TWENTYONESWINGS 40a - "Playground situation #2." TWENTYTHREEKIDS "1962 Robert Mitchum /Shirley Maclaine or the outcome of 17 and 40 across?" TWOFORTHESEESAW Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=9/16/2009&g=64&d=A ...
The D and S are circled in "BEDSLATS" but bedslats are not called "D's" as far as I know.

Heavens to Betsy, that was an impressive puzzle! I used to play The Three R's for my kids and my ESL students on Earth Day. I think that helped me to get the theme almost instantly. https://youtu.be/USo_vH1Jz7E?si=vVJp74yL3c9Tox7F The original School House Rock song is a stone cold classic, as is the De La Soul number. https://youtu.be/J8lRKCw2_Pk?si=yml8fYHAPqV36mQY https://youtu.be/pxkOWjZAPLs?si=aeNmIPJL2QbLa2q8
Might have been better to leave out the shaded and circled squares, and just let solvers enjoy the Earth Day CANs and RECYCLEs and REUSEs and all?
I kept getting the feelling that this puzzle really wanted to be a rebus.
WHAT DO YOH MEAN JOHN CAN?

Oh.. what the heck - one more late puzzle find. Just thought this one was kind of cute. Anyway - a Tuesday from April 6, 2021 by Amanda Chung and Karl Ni. Theme answers in that one: HEAVENSTOBETSY GEEZLOUISE GOODGOLLY MISSMOLLY Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=4/6/2021&g=16&d=A ....
Maybe one of the coolest and most holistically constructed theme puzzles I've ever played. So fun, such a joy!
Wow
Bravo and thank you!
Wow!! So impressed! Loved the interpretation of each of the theme words and a good reminder for us all. Happy Earth Day! 🌎

BETtY crossing ENOt was a tough one for someone not so familiar with biblical names. Finally got my reward upon changing that t to an S. Close call today! Loved the puzzle other than that sticky spot!
with this many themes it strikes me as more of a Thursday despite being decently easy—Betsy tripped me up though, I was so sure it was Betty and didn’t know better for the Genesis grandson

This was a very enjoyable puzzle and I was able to solve it 5 minutes faster than my usual Wednesday, and that on little sleep as it was (albeit with some Google-fu for the actor/actress names for which I am totally clueless). I was, however, rather disappointed that LOUCHE was not in the thesaurus. And I have never known a Liz who's nickname was BETSY, so that one threw me for a minute.

This was a tough Wednesday! I was just commenting down below that I was stumped in the middle west...I finally just looked up the Tony award-winning Hagan and the NBA star Jeremy...and my guess for the K-group turned out to be right, so voila: puzzle finally finished. Aside from my struggles in the midwest, I did enjoy the puzzle! I loved that my hero, Jane Goodall, was in it! Great Earth Day theme, and very clever all the way around.
This was so cool!!

My word did I hate the SE corner of this one like nothing in quite awhile. I had YELP for 54A for the longest time and repeatedly went back and forth with GLASS at 64A. That corner tripped me up for a half hour.
The use of the theme is awesome.  Gave a great sense of accomplishment when I finished and figured it out.  Some risk of such an endeavor coming across as contrived but no such concern here.  Brava!
This is so epic! Props for an incredible construction, and happy Earth Day!
Did anyone notice a couple answers were REUSED from Connections and Strands? Wink 😉
This was super clever. Wow.

Once again I'm very late to comment, but for the second day in a row I can't let this one go without a note of congratulations! 

My solving m.o. is to start before bedtime after the puzzle has dropped, then finish up over breakfast when needed. So I was much more widely awake solving this one midday Wednesday — and yet it was frustratingly blank after my first pass. I wasn't remotely on the wavelength, and super annoyed! (Grumpily grumbling in my head about all the fussy circles and shaded squares, to boot.) 

And then, down there with HELEN of Troy, slowly the theme – and with it, a flash of recognition for what day on the calendar it was – bloomed into being in my grid. Really superb!!! I lingered over the finished work, savoring all the very clever ripples of the theme and the way those three words were used to great effect. Plus Ms. Goodall! Just wow.

Whatever is the environmentalist's equivalent of a chef's kiss, that's for you, Hoang-Kim Vu!
@Fact Boy yet in France, they can can can…

Anywhere, there was a lot of back and forth.  Good people on both sides, in my opinion.

As far as I can tell, the entire thread was taken down sometime last night or this morning.

@Francis I think I speak for the majority when I say you have no enemies here. Your reply to another poster here today (“Are you new here?”) made me love you even more than your detailed chemistry posts which I find endearing even though they are meaningless to me.

userLocation: "How many divisions does the pope have?"
"How many divisions does the pope have?"
commentBody:
@Francis
Yes, but at some point your ability to prove that 2^(.5) cannot be expressed by any fraction was valued enough by somebody that they paid you for that bundle of skills, a payment that eventually funded your purchase of plumbing services. 
The really important part of this story is the invention of money. This alleviated the need to find A the builder who was worried about square roots and had cabbages to sell. You could help A out in exchange for cabbages of which you had no need but maybe B the tailor did. So you exchanged your cabbages for, say, some ties.  When you heard that the retired plumber C had a daughter who was getting married, eureka! Here was a man in need of ties who could fix a leak. Of course, by now your house is adrift on its soggy foundations...
So the real heroine is the person who said some millennia ago, "Hmm, what we need is a standard medium of exchange..."

@SBK in TO

😂  Precisely.

I sometime wonder at how little we (I) know about the complicated web of interactions needed to provide us (me) with an environment that we're (I'm) at least mostly comfortable with.  

There a line from Leonard Cohen's "Suzanne" ...

"And she feeds you tea and oranges that come all the way from China"
...

It took a reviewer to point out to me that a *lot* of our fruit comes "all the way from China".😀

@Francis

I didn’t get to see the comments yesterday,

but I know that would have enjoyed your
dashingly handsome, sharp wit 
and defended your honor.
@Francis Home ownership is an adventure, isn't it? So many little things that balloon into big things if you don't tend to them right away. And sometimes if you DO tend to them right away 😬

@Francis

The important thing is to know how to turn off the water main coming into the house --in a hurry. Something I learned the hard way. But haven't forgotten.

@Francis

Actually, there was a great comment about what is wrong with legitimizing ANTIFA as some kind of extremist organization that was very well worded, which I praised for getting to the heart of the issue in just three lines.

I have been using the new search feature to look up my own posts, to see if there have been any replies I've wanted to address, and I saw that my comment on that thread was gone.  Upon further examination, I saw that the entire thread was gone.

There was nothing offensive about any of it, and other threads on the same topic remained.

Go figure around here.

@SBK in TO

This reminds me of the plot of at least two M*A*S*H episodes…

@Heidi
I suspect one of them was the wrangle to get tomato juice for Col. Potter. But I can't recall the other. Help, please.

@Francis
I am going to grab this a hook for a wise saying. It's from a book I think had "excellence" in the title and maybe the author was  "Gardiner" (yeah I could look it up but I'm leaving the exercise to the student)..
"A society that tolerates the second-rate* in philosophers because philosophy is an exalted subject and in its plumbers because plumbing is a lowly subject is a society in trouble: neither its pipes nor its theories will hold water."
*might have been "shoddiness"

@RozzieGrandma Racing in to report that the author is John W. Gardner, the quote appears to be one of those common success/standards/ goals/ideals bywords and is widely available, and it can be found here: https://www.pbs.org/johngardner/sections/writings.html He does have a bunch of witty things to say, quoted on the PBS page.

@Steve L

Maybe too much? I can’t say I’m not impressed, but I’m also reminded of Coco Chanel’s advice to remove one accessory before leaving the house. Sometimes less is more.

@Heidi Agreed. It was impressively constructed, but the opposite of the nice flowy feeling that really great (to me) crosswords have. I found it a bit ...overstimulating? Tiresome? with all the highlighting and circled letters in the kangaroo words.

@Heidi

I gave my opinion not as praise, not as criticism, just as a simple statement. You can do with it what you wish.

@Steve L
Didn't she?

commentBody:
@john ezra
Thank you for the wonderful excerpt from Agee. More apposite every day, I'm sorry to say.

@john ezra Just recently saw one of the beautiful images from that project at an exhibit at the MAG in Rochester, NY. The image itself spoke volumes, but the beauty of the black and white print was also breathtaking. I forgot how entirely moving that project was. And when all we see now is crappy digital imagery, it was a joy to revel in a real photographic gem.

@Bill
I REUSE and RECYCLE almost everything from my garden. Lawn clippings make great mulch. Spent plants are recycled into A-1 compost. Even yogurt containers make flower pots, along with supermarket reusable tote bags. I thought of composting the junk mail but stopped at that. Fall leaves are gardeners' gold. Happy planting.
For those who come here to savor the clues and answers, not to speed through the grid, it was very clever during the solve.

@Geoff Offermann 
They drop worldwide when it's Midnight in New York.  

I was lucky enough to spend all of last December in Australia and New Zealand. Being on the other side of the International Date line, and variously 15, 16 or 18 hours ahead (I was in 3 different time zones in Australia/NZ), made it pretty difficult to keep the various NYT puzzle times straight!

Midnight for some, and either 10pm or 6pm for the Crosswords, depending on the day of the week in NY - which of course was a different day where I was.
I *just barely* managed to keep track of things enough to maintain my Crossword and Wordle streaks! (The streaks were most at risk on the more-than-24-travel-time days).

I did miss a few days of Spelling Bee, Strands and Connections, but I don't care as much about those.

I've thrown my back out at some of the most inopportune times, but you've surpassed any place I've ever done so (even in New Jersey)
Rest up and get well.

@Helen Wright 

I can't cite anything to support this, but I suspect that here in the United States the name "Betty" is seen as out of date, and "Betsy" even more so. I've encountered  several women who have had to endure having "Elizabeth" converted, unasked, into one of these.

I have a family member who went to great lengths to get everyone to quit calling her "Betty," preferring "Beth;" she eventually succeeded, but it took years of insisting and correcting.

My wife had an Aunt Elizabeth who owned a small  store in rural Alabama, which she called "Elizabeth D_____ Market."  Had she used the full "Elizabeth" on the sign in front it would have required small enough letters to make the sign difficult to read from the road, but rather than submit to being  "Betty" she just abbreviated "Elizabeth" to "ELIZ." on the sign.

@Dale M Unfortunately only positive reviews seem to be allowed these days. My comments continue to be blocked.

@D 
I've seen negative reviews on many puzzles... I don't think that's why your comments are blocked.

@D Meanwhile, being very negative toward anyone who might  have the slightest nit to pick, and doing so in either a condescending or superior minded way, seems heartily encouraged. We should all strive to be more Minnesota Nice as opposed to Wordplay Nice.

They are really trying hard to turn this comment section into the Everybody Loves Everybody Club. Sometimes people just aren't crazy about a particular puzzle. That isn't a crime that seems to necessitate being banished from the kingdom.

@lawrenceb56

The problem arises when the complaints are blatantly off-base in some objective way.  

One of the most frequent is the "That is not a word" type.  It is so easy to check a dictionary these days.

Personal insults aimed at constructors and editors should be condemned.

@lawrenceb56 - All of us have complained about puzzles at one time or another. But a lot of the new folks seem to think this is Facebook, so they just post whatever hate they can, no matter how wrong-headed, and expect to be praised for their "wit." 

Most often it is clear that they are just not very good solvers, especially when they claim that a word is not a word (when it is), or claim that since THEY have never heard of it, it can't possibly exist (when many of us know the word or phrase in question), or end their unsupported diatribe with "do better" -- that's a sure sign of an off-base comment. 

Criticisms that are based in reality and supported with examples may be rebutted, but they're not generally treated with the same level of scorn that a comment such as "Nobody says "XXXX"" when "XXXX" is something many of us have heard plenty of times. Or just "this puzzle sucks" with nothing to back up that asinine claim. 

If you want criticisms to be accepted and respected, perhaps you need to come up with some valid criticisms. This comment section ain't your typical FB group where trolls outnumber intelligent people.

@Barry Ancona "Web" is short for the "World Wide Web." Type "https://nyt.com" into your "browser" and click "Games" then "The Crossword."

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight those clues are palindromes, aren't they? the second half of the word reuses the letters in the first half.

@Cherry
I think what is meant is that the full answers to those clues are words, while the shaded squares are anagrams of the unshaded squares in the same answer. PUL and LUP, etc, not that the 
letters in all of the sets of shaded squares also form words by themselves, although some do.
